question_hi: मकान मालिक डिपॉजिट नहीं लौटा रहा
answer_hi: किराया नियंत्रण अधिनियम के तहत, मकान मालिक को लीज खत्म होने पर डिपॉजिट वापस करना होगा। आप रेंट कंट्रोलर के पास शिकायत दर्ज करा सकते हैं।
answer_ta: வாடகைக் கட்டுப்பாட்டுச் சட்டத்தின் கீழ், குத்தகை முடிவடைந்தவுடன் நில உரிமையாளர் வைப்புத்தொகையைத் திருப்பித் தர வேண்டும். நீங்கள் வாடகைக் கட்டுப்பாட்டாளரிடம் புகார் அளிக்கலாம்.
answer_en: Under the Rent Control Act, landlords must return the deposit upon lease termination. You can file a complaint with the Rent Controller.
case_study: V. Dhanapal Chettiar vs. Yesodai Ammal (1979): SC held that tenant eviction must follow strictly statutory grounds.

question_hi: पुलिस एफआईआर दर्ज नहीं कर रही है
answer_hi: यदि पुलिस एफआईआर दर्ज करने से मना करती है, तो आप एसपी को लिखित शिकायत भेज सकते हैं या सीआरपीसी धारा 156(3) के तहत मजिस्ट्रेट से संपर्क कर सकते हैं।
answer_ta: காவல்துறை எப்ஐஆர் பதிவு செய்ய மறுத்தால், நீங்கள் எஸ்பிக்கு எழுத்துப்பூர்வ புகார் அனுப்பலாம் அல்லது சிஆர்பிசி பிரிவு 156(3) இன் கீழ் மாஜிஸ்திரேட்டை அணுகலாம்.
answer_en: If police refuse to file FIR, you can send a written complaint to the SP or approach a Magistrate under CrPC Section 156(3).
case_study: Lalita Kumari vs. Govt. of U.P. (2014): Supreme Court ruled that registration of FIR is mandatory if information discloses commission of a cognizable offence.

answer_hi: घरेलू हिंसा अधिनियम 2005 के तहत आप सुरक्षा अधिकारी या पुलिस से संपर्क कर सकती हैं।
answer_ta: குடும்ப வன்முறைச் சட்டம் 2005 இன் கீழ் நீங்கள் பாதுகாப்பு அதிகாரியையோ அல்லது காவல்துறையையோ அணுகலாம்.
answer_en: Under the Domestic Violence Act 2005, you can approach a Protection Officer or the Police.
case_study: S.R. Batra vs. Taruna Batra (2007): Interpreted rights of women to shared household under Domestic Violence Act.

answer_hi: वेतन भुगतान अधिनियम 1936 के तहत आप श्रम आयुक्त के पास दावा दायर कर सकते हैं।
answer_ta: ஊதியக் கொடுப்பனவுச் சட்டம், 1936 இன் கீழ் நீங்கள் தொழிலாளர் ஆணையரிடம் கோரிக்கை வைக்கலாம்.
answer_en: You can file a claim with the Labour Commissioner under the Payment of Wages Act 1936.
case_study: People’s Union for Democratic Rights vs. Union of India (1982): Non-payment of minimum wages amounts to 'forced labour' prohibited under Article 23.

question_hi: सरकारी काम या फंड की जानकारी चाहिए
question_ta: அரசு வேலை அல்லது நிதி பற்றிய தகவல் தேவை
question_en: Need info on government work or funds
answer_hi: आरटीआई अधिनियम 2005 के तहत आप किसी भी सार्वजनिक प्राधिकरण से जानकारी मांग सकते हैं।
answer_ta: தகவல் அறியும் உரிமைச் சட்டம் 2005 இன் கீழ் நீங்கள் எந்தவொரு பொது அதிகாரியிடமிருந்தும் தகவல்களைக் கோரலாம்.
answer_en: Under RTI Act 2005, you can request information from any public authority.
case_study: CBSE vs. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011): Right to Information is part of fundamental right to freedom of speech.

question_hi: गिरफ्तारी के समय क्या अधिकार हैं?
question_ta: கைது செய்யும் போது என்ன உரிமைகள் உள்ளன?
answer_hi: डी.के. बसु दिशानिर्देशों के अनुसार, पुलिस को गिरफ्तारी का कारण बताना होगा और आपके परिवार को सूचित करना होगा।
answer_ta: டி.கே. பாசு வழிகாட்டுதல்களின்படி, காவல்துறை கைதுக்கான காரணத்தைக் கூற வேண்டும் மற்றும் உங்கள் குடும்பத்தினருக்குத் தெரிவிக்க வேண்டும்.
answer_en: As per D.K. Basu guidelines, police must inform grounds of arrest and notify your family.
citation: D.K. Basu vs State of West Bengal
case_study: D.K. Basu vs. State of West Bengal (1997): Laid down specific guidelines to be followed by police during arrest and detention to prevent custodial torture.

answer_hi: उपभोक्ता संरक्षण अधिनियम 2019 के तहत आप उपभोक्ता फोरम में शिकायत कर सकते हैं।
answer_ta: நுகர்வோர் பாதுகாப்புச் சட்டம் 2019 இன் கீழ் நீங்கள் நுகர்வோர் மன்றத்தில் புகார் செய்யலாம்.
answer_en: You can file a complaint in Consumer Forum under Consumer Protection Act 2019.
case_study: Indian Medical Association vs. V.P. Shantha (1995): Rendered medical profession within the ambit of 'service' under Consumer Protection Act.

question_hi: क्या कानून सबके लिए बराबर है?
answer_hi: संविधान का अनुच्छेद 14 कानून के समक्ष समानता की गारंटी देता है।
answer_ta: அரசமைப்பின் 14 வது பிரிவு சட்டத்தின் முன் அனைவரும் சமம் என்பதற்கு உத்தரவாதம் அளிக்கிறது.
answer_en: Article 14 of the Constitution guarantees equality before the law.
case_study: Maneka Gandhi vs. Union of India (1978): Expanded the scope of Article 14, 19 and 21, creating the 'Gold Triangle' of fundamental rights.

answer_hi: अनुच्छेद 21 जीवन और व्यक्तिगत स्वतंत्रता की सुरक्षा सुनिश्चित करता है।
answer_ta: பிரிவு 21 வாழ்க்கை மற்றும் தனிப்பட்ட சுதந்திரத்திற்கான பாதுகாப்பை உறுதி செய்கிறது.
answer_en: Article 21 ensures protection of life and personal liberty.
case_study: Olga Tellis vs. Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985): Right to livelihood is part of Right to Life under Article 21.

question_hi: महिला के साथ छेड़छाड़ की सजा
answer_hi: आईपीसी धारा 354 के तहत, महिला की लज्जा भंग करने के इरादे से हमला करना दंडनीय अपराध है (1-5 साल की कैद)।
answer_ta: ஐபிசி பிரிவு 354 இன் படி, ஒரு பெண்ணின் கண்ணியத்தை அவமதிக்கும் நோக்கில் தாக்குதல் நடத்துவது தண்டனைக்குரிய குற்றமாகும்.
answer_en: Under IPC Section 354, assault with intent to outrage modesty/molestation is punishable (1-5 years jail).
case_study: Vishaka vs. State of Rajasthan (1997): Provided guidelines to prevent sexual harassment at workplace (later became POSH Act).

question_hi: ऑनलाइन धोखाधड़ी की शिकायत कहां करें?
question_ta: ஆன்லைன் மோசடி பற்றி எங்கு புகார் அளிப்பது?
answer_hi: आप cybercrime.gov.in पर या 1930 हेल्पलाइन पर कॉल करके शिकायत दर्ज कर सकते हैं।
answer_ta: நீங்கள் cybercrime.gov.in இல் அல்லது 1930 ஹெல்ப்லைன் எண்ணில் புகார் செய்யலாம்.
answer_en: You can report at cybercrime.gov.in or call 1930 helpline.
case_study: Shreya Singhal vs. Union of India (2015): Struck down Section 66A of IT Act affecting online free speech.

answer_hi: 14 वर्ष से कम उम्र के बच्चों से काम करवाना प्रतिबंधित है (बाल श्रम निषेध अधिनियम)।
answer_en: Employing children under 14 is prohibited (Child Labor Prohibition Act).
case_study: M.C. Mehta vs. State of Tamil Nadu (1996): Supreme Court guidelines for rehabilitation of child labourers.

question_ta: என் காலேஜ்ல என்னை செக்ஸுவல் அராஸ் பண்றாங்க என்ன பண்றதுன்னு தெரியல
answer_hi: रैगिंग एक गंभीर अपराध है। यूजीसी हेल्पलाइन 1800-180-5522 पर कॉल करें। सुप्रीम कोर्ट (विश्व जागरण बनाम भारत संघ) ने इसे प्रतिबंधित किया है।
answer_ta: ராகிங் மற்றும் பாலியல் துன்புறுத்தல் ஒரு கடுமையான குற்றம். உடனடியாக உங்கள் கல்லூரி நிர்வாகத்திடமும், காவல் நிலையத்திலும் (FIR) புகார் அளிக்கவும். UGC Anti-Ragging உதவி எண்: 1800-180-5522.
answer_en:
Ragging is a criminal offense strictly prohibited in India by the Supreme Court. Under the **UGC Regulations on Curbing the Menace of Ragging in Higher Educational Institutions (2009)**, all educational institutions are legally mandated to maintain a ragging-free campus.

**Key Legal Information:**
1.  **Supreme Court Mandate:** Based on the 2009 SC judgment (*University of Kerala v. Council, Principals' Colleges*), ragging is a non-bailable offense.
2.  **Liability:** College management can be held liable and face withdrawal of affiliation if they fail to take action against reported incidents.
3.  **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS):** Depending on the severity, acts of ragging can be prosecuted under sections related to criminal intimidation, hurt, or wrongful restraint (formerly under IPC).
4.  **Helpline:** You can report ragging to the **UGC Anti-Ragging Helpline at 1800-180-5522** or via email at **helpline@antiragging.in**.

If you are a victim, you should immediately file a formal complaint with the college's Anti-Ragging Committee and the local police station.
case_study: University of Kerala vs. Council of Principals (2009): SC laid down strict anti-ragging guidelines making institutions accountable.

answer_hi: निःशुल्क कानूनी सेवाओं के लिए आप नालसा हेल्पलाइन 15100 पर कॉल कर सकते हैं या www.nalsa.gov.in पर जा सकते हैं।
answer_ta: இலவச சட்ட சேவைகளுக்கு நீங்கள் NALSA உதவி எண் 15100 ஐ அழைக்கலாம் அல்லது www.nalsa.gov.in ஐப் பார்வையிடலாம்.
answer_en: You can call NALSA Helpline at 15100 or visit www.nalsa.gov.in for free legal services.

question_en: I am dragging from my college so what can I do for that
answer_en:
If you are referring to **ragging** (harassment or bullying in an educational institution), it is a serious criminal offense in India. Here is what you can do under the law:

### 1. Report to College Authorities
Under the **UGC Regulations on Curbing the Menace of Ragging in Higher Educational Institutions (2009)**, every college must have an Anti-Ragging Committee and an Anti-Ragging Squad. You should immediately file a formal written complaint with them.

### 2. Contact National Helpline
You can report the incident anonymously to the **National Anti-Ragging Helpline**:
*   **Phone:** 1800-180-5522 (Toll-free, 24/7)
*   **Email:** helpline@antiragging.in

### 3. File a Police Complaint (FIR)
Ragging involves acts that are punishable under the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)** (now transitioned to the **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita - BNS**). Depending on the nature of the act, the following sections may apply:
*   **Section 323 IPC (BNS 115):** Voluntarily causing hurt.
*   **Section 341 IPC (BNS 126):** Wrongful restraint.
*   **Section 506 IPC (BNS 351):** Criminal intimidation.
*   **Section 354 IPC:** If the ragging involves outraging modesty (as mentioned in your context).

### 4. State-Specific Laws
Many states have specific laws, such as the **Maharashtra Prohibition of Ragging Act, 1999** or the **Tamil Nadu Prohibition of Ragging Act, 1997**, which provide for imprisonment and mandatory expulsion from the institution.

**Recommendation:** Document all incidents (dates, times, names of perpetrators) and seek immediate assistance from your college principal or the local police station.

question_en: I got arrested but I didn't do anything what should I do
answer_en:
If you have been arrested, you are protected by specific fundamental rights under the **Constitution of India** and procedural safeguards under the **Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS)**, which has replaced the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC).

Here is the legal information regarding your rights and the steps you should take:

### 1. Fundamental Rights (Constitution of India)
*   **Article 22(1):** You have the right to be informed of the grounds for your arrest.
*   **Article 22(1):** You have the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner (lawyer) of your choice.
*   **Article 22(2):** You must be produced before the nearest Magistrate within **24 hours** of your arrest (excluding travel time).

### 2. Rights under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS)
*   **Right to Know Grounds of Arrest (Section 35):** The police officer must communicate full particulars of the offense or other grounds for the arrest.
*   **Right to Inform a Relative (Section 36):** The police are legally obligated to inform a relative or friend nominated by you about your arrest and the place where you are being held.
*   **Right to Legal Counsel (Section 35):** You have the right to meet an advocate of your choice during interrogation, though not throughout the entire interrogation.
*   **Medical Examination (Section 53):** You have the right to be examined by a medical officer immediately after arrest to record any injuries or your physical condition.

### 3. If the Arrest is Unlawful
If you believe you have been arrested without cause or wrongly:
*   **Bail:** You can apply for bail through your lawyer. If the offense is "bailable," you are entitled to bail as a matter of right.
*   **Quashing the FIR:** Your lawyer can approach the High Court under **Section 528 of the BNSS** (formerly Section 482 CrPC) to quash the First Information Report (FIR) if there is no evidence against you.
*   **Habeas Corpus:** If you are detained beyond 24 hours without being produced before a Magistrate, a writ of *Habeas Corpus* can be filed in the High Court or Supreme Court.

### 4. Immediate Steps to Take:
1.  **Stay Calm:** Do not resist arrest physically, as this can lead to additional charges (e.g., obstructing a public servant).
2.  **Ask for the Grounds:** Formally ask the arresting officer why you are being arrested and request to see the **Arrest Memo**.
3.  **Silence:** You have the right against self-incrimination (Article 20(3)). You are not required to sign any blank papers or confessions under pressure.
4.  **Contact a Lawyer:** Immediately ask to speak with your legal representative or have your family contact one.

**Note:** If you cannot afford a lawyer, you are entitled to **Free Legal Aid** under the Legal Services Authorities Act. You should inform the Magistrate about this when produced in court.

question_ta: நீ காலேஜ்ல என்னை செக்ஸுவல் ஹராஸ் பண்றாங்க என்ன பண்றதுன்னு தெரியல கொஞ்சம் ஐடியா கொடு
answer_hi:
घरेलू हिंसा के मामले में आप निम्नलिखित कानूनी कदम उठा सकती हैं:

1. **घरेलू हिंसा से महिलाओं का संरक्षण अधिनियम, 2005 (Domestic Violence Act 2005):** इस कानून के तहत आप मजिस्ट्रेट के पास आवेदन कर सुरक्षा आदेश (Protection Order) या निवास का अधिकार प्राप्त कर सकती हैं। आप इसके लिए अपने क्षेत्र के सुरक्षा अधिकारी (Protection Officer) से भी संपर्क कर सकती हैं।

2. **भारतीय न्याय संहिता (BNS) की धारा 85 (पूर्व में IPC की धारा 498A):** पति या उसके रिश्तेदारों द्वारा की गई शारीरिक या मानसिक क्रूरता के विरुद्ध आप पुलिस स्टेशन में प्राथमिकी (FIR) दर्ज करा सकती हैं।

3. **तत्काल सहायता:** आपातकालीन स्थिति में आप पुलिस हेल्पलाइन नंबर **112** या महिला हेल्पलाइन नंबर **181** पर कॉल कर सकती हैं।

आप कानूनी सलाह के लिए जिला विधिक सेवा प्राधिकरण (DLSA) से भी संपर्क कर सकती हैं जहाँ मुफ्त कानूनी सहायता उपलब्ध होती है।
answer_ta:
கல்லூரியில் ராகிங் மற்றும் பாலியல் துன்புறுத்தல் என்பது ஒரு கடுமையான குற்றமாகும். நீங்கள் உடனடியாகப் பின்வரும் நடவடிக்கைகளை எடுக்க வேண்டும்:

1. **கல்லூரி நிர்வாகத்திடம் புகார் அளிக்கவும்:** உங்கள் கல்லூரியின் உள் புகார் குழு (Internal Complaints Committee - ICC) அல்லது ராகிங் எதிர்ப்பு குழுவிடம் (Anti-Ragging Committee) எழுத்துப்பூர்வமாக புகார் அளிக்கவும்.
2. **காவல்துறையில் புகார் அளிக்கவும்:** அருகில் உள்ள காவல் நிலையத்தில் உடனடியாக முதல் தகவல் அறிக்கை (FIR) பதிவு செய்யவும்.
3. **உதவி எண்:** UGC-யின் ராகிங் எதிர்ப்பு உதவி எண்ணைத் தொடர்பு கொள்ளவும்: **1800-180-5522**.

**சட்டப் பிரிவு:** UGC Anti-Ragging Regulations, 2009 மற்றும் பாரதிய நியாய சன்ஹிதா (BNS) பிரிவு 74 மற்றும் 79.

question_hi: आईपीसी धारा 354 के तहत, महिला की लज्जा भंग करने के इरादे से हमला करना दंडनीय अपराध है (1-5 साल की कैद)।",
answer_hi:
भारतीय दंड संहिता (IPC) की धारा 354 के अनुसार, यदि कोई व्यक्ति किसी महिला की लज्जा भंग करने के इरादे से उस पर हमला करता है या आपराधिक बल का प्रयोग करता है, तो यह एक गंभीर कानूनी अपराध है।

**कानूनी विवरण:**
1. **सजा:** इस धारा के तहत दोषी पाए जाने पर न्यूनतम 1 वर्ष और अधिकतम 5 वर्ष तक के कारावास के साथ जुर्माने का प्रावधान है।
2. **प्रकृति:** यह एक संज्ञेय (Cognizable) और गैर-जमानती (Non-bailable) अपराध है।
3. **नया कानून (BNS):** नए भारतीय कानूनों के तहत, अब इसे **भारतीय न्याय संहिता (BNS) की धारा 74** के रूप में जाना जाता है।

यह कानून महिलाओं की सुरक्षा और उनके सम्मान की रक्षा के लिए बनाया गया है।

answer_en:
Under **Section 354 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC)**, the punishment for molestation (assault or criminal force to a woman with intent to outrage her modesty) is imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than one year but which may extend to **five years**, and the offender shall also be liable to a fine.

Under the new **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)**, this provision is covered under **Section 74**, carrying similar penalties.

It appears you are referring to **"Eve-teasing,"** which is a term commonly used in India to describe public sexual harassment of women. This is a serious criminal offense under Indian law.

Under the **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)**, 2023, and the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**, the following sections apply:

1.  **Section 79 of BNS (formerly Section 509 of IPC):** This section deals with any word, gesture, or act intended to insult the modesty of a woman. It includes making sounds, exhibiting objects, or intruding upon a woman's privacy. 
    *   **Punishment:** Up to 3 years of imprisonment and a fine.

2.  **Section 74 of BNS (formerly Section 354 of IPC):** This covers assault or the use of criminal force against a woman with the intent to outrage her modesty.
    *   **Punishment:** Imprisonment for a term between 1 to 5 years and a fine.

3.  **Section 78 of BNS (formerly Section 354D of IPC):** This pertains to **Stalking**, which includes following a woman or contacting her repeatedly despite her clear indication of disinterest, or monitoring her use of the internet/electronic communication.
    *   **Punishment:** Up to 3 years for the first conviction and up to 5 years for subsequent convictions.

4.  **Information Technology Act, 2000:** If the harassment occurs online (Cyber-stalking or sending obscene messages), sections like **Section 67** may be invoked.

In addition to central laws, several states have specific acts, such as the **Tamil Nadu Prohibition of Harassment of Woman Act, 1998**, which provide stricter penalties for eve-teasing.

"Eve-teasing" is a colloquial term used in India to describe various forms of sexual harassment or street harassment of women. While the term is not explicitly defined in the law, these acts are strictly prohibited and penalized under the **Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)**, which replaced the Indian Penal Code (IPC) effective July 1, 2024.

The relevant legal provisions are:

### 1. Insulting the Modesty of a Woman
*   **BNS Section 79 (formerly IPC Section 509):** This section deals with words, gestures, sounds, or the exhibition of objects intended to insult the modesty of a woman or intrude upon her privacy.
*   **Punishment:** Up to 3 years of imprisonment and a fine.

### 2. Sexual Harassment
*   **BNS Section 75 (formerly IPC Section 354A):** This covers unwelcome physical contact, demands for sexual favors, making sexually colored remarks, or showing pornography against a woman's will.
*   **Punishment:** Depending on the severity, 1 to 3 years of imprisonment and/or a fine.

### 3. Use of Criminal Force to Outrage Modesty
*   **BNS Section 74 (formerly IPC Section 354):** This applies if the offender uses assault or criminal force with the intent to outrage a woman's modesty.
*   **Punishment:** Imprisonment between 1 to 5 years and a fine.

### 4. Stalking
*   **BNS Section 78 (formerly IPC Section 354D):** If the harassment involves following a woman, attempting to contact her repeatedly despite her clear indication of disinterest, or monitoring her use of the internet/electronic communication.
*   **Punishment:** Up to 3 years for the first conviction and up to 5 years for subsequent convictions.

### 5. State-Specific Laws
Several Indian states have enacted specific legislation to curb eve-teasing. For example:
*   **The Tamil Nadu Prohibition of Harassment of Woman Act, 1998:** Provides stringent penalties for harassment in public places.

**Procedural Note:** Under the **Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS)**, victims can file a "Zero FIR" at any police station regardless of where the incident occurred, ensuring immediate legal action.

Under Indian law, the **Right to Life** is a fundamental right guaranteed by **Article 21 of the Constitution of India**. 

It states that "no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law." This right is available to both citizens and non-citizens and has been interpreted by the Judiciary to include the right to live with human dignity and all the necessities of life.

question_en: police is not filing the fir
answer_en:
If the police refuse to register an FIR for a cognizable offense, you have the following legal remedies under the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) and the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS):

1. **Written Complaint to the Superintendent of Police (SP):**
   Under **Section 154(3) of the CrPC** (now **Section 173(4) of the BNSS**), you can send your complaint in writing to the SP via registered post. If the SP is satisfied that a cognizable offense is disclosed, they will either investigate the matter personally or direct an investigation by a subordinate officer.

2. **Approach the Judicial Magistrate:**
   If the police still fail to act, you can file a complaint before a Judicial Magistrate under **Section 156(3) of the CrPC** (now **Section 175 of the BNSS**). The Magistrate has the power to order the police to register the FIR and initiate an investigation.

3. **Zero FIR:**
   Note that police are legally bound to register a "Zero FIR" regardless of their jurisdiction if a cognizable offense is reported, which is then transferred to the relevant police station.

**Relevant Laws:** 
* Section 154 and 156(3) of the CrPC.
* Section 173 and 175 of the BNSS.

Ragging is a serious criminal offense in India and is strictly prohibited. If you are being subjected to ragging in your college, you have several legal protections and avenues for recourse:

### 1. UGC Regulations
Under the **UGC Regulations on Curbing the Menace of Ragging in Higher Educational Institutions, 2009**, all colleges are legally required to:
*   Have a functional **Anti-Ragging Committee** and **Anti-Ragging Squad**.
*   Take immediate action and, if the nature of the ragging is criminal, file an FIR with the local police within 24 hours.

### 2. Legal Provisions under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS)
Specific acts of ragging fall under various sections of the BNS:
*   **Section 115:** Voluntarily causing hurt.
*   **Section 126:** Wrongful restraint.
*   **Section 351:** Criminal intimidation.
*   **Section 108:** Abetment of suicide (in extreme cases).

### 3. State-Specific Laws
Many states have their own strict laws, such as the **Tamil Nadu Prohibition of Ragging Act, 1997**, or the **Maharashtra Prohibition of Ragging Act, 1999**, which carry penalties of imprisonment and expulsion from the institution.

### 4. Steps You Should Take:
1.  **Report to the College:** Submit a written complaint to your college's Anti-Ragging Committee or the Principal.
2.  **National Anti-Ragging Helpline:** Call the 24/7 toll-free helpline at **1800-180-5522** or email **helpline@antiragging.in**.
3.  **File an FIR:** You can visit the nearest police station to file a First Information Report (FIR) against the perpetrators.
4.  **Online Complaint:** You can register a complaint anonymously or otherwise at **[www.antiragging.in](https://www.antiragging.in)**.

If the college authorities fail to take action, they themselves can be held liable for negligence under the directions of the Supreme Court of India.

In India, tenant rights are protected under state-specific **Rent Control Acts**, the **Transfer of Property Act, 1882**, and the **Model Tenancy Act, 2021** (which is being progressively adopted by various states). 

Here are the key rights of a tenant under Indian law:

### 1. Protection Against Arbitrary Eviction
A landlord cannot evict a tenant without a valid legal reason (such as non-payment of rent, breach of tenancy terms, or subletting without consent). To evict a tenant, the landlord must serve a formal written notice as per the rent agreement or under **Section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882**.

### 2. Right to Essential Services
Landlords are legally prohibited from cutting off essential services—such as water supply, electricity, passage, stairs, or sanitary services—to force a tenant to vacate, even during a dispute. Doing so is a punishable offense under state Rent Control Acts.

### 3. Right to Privacy
A tenant has the right to quiet enjoyment of the property. The landlord cannot enter the rented premises without prior notice and reasonable justification, except in case of emergencies.

### 4. Cap on Security Deposit
Under the **Model Tenancy Act, 2021**, the security deposit for residential premises is capped at a maximum of **two months' rent**, and for non-residential (commercial) premises, it is capped at a maximum of six months' rent. This deposit must be refunded upon the termination of the tenancy, after deducting outstanding dues.

### 5. Right to Maintenance and Repairs
The landlord is responsible for keeping the premises in a habitable condition and carrying out major structural repairs. If the landlord fails to make necessary repairs, the tenant may carry them out and deduct the expenses from the rent, subject to prior notice and agreement.

### 6. Protection Against Unfair Rent Hikes
Rent cannot be increased arbitrarily. Any increase must be in accordance with the terms laid down in the registered Rent Agreement or the provisions of the local Rent Control Act.

**Recommendation:** Always ensure that a **Rent Agreement** is registered (mandatory for tenancies exceeding 11 months under the Registration Act, 1908) to legally secure these rights.
